coredata.dc:description: Bisphosphonates (BPs) are some of the most widely used drugs in osteoporosis (OP) treatment, but they can cause severe side effects such as bisphosphonate-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (BRONJ), causing great pain in patients. It is necessary to develop a novel drug for treating OP with fewer side effects. In this study, novel carbon dots, ALEN-CDs, derived from polyethylene glycol (PEG) and alendronate (ALEN) are developed. ALEN-CDs have good biocompatibility, photoluminescence (PL) properties, and bone-targeting ability. They can inhibit the differentiation and maturation of osteoclasts, play an immunoregulatory role by inhibiting macrophage polarization to the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype, and promote polarization to the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype. It is worth noting that compared with ALEN, high-dose applications of ALEN-CDs do not lead to BRONJ, which may be related to the regulatory effect on M2 macrophage polarization. Moreover, in vivo studies show that ALEN-CDs significantly reduce bone loss and improve OP in ovariectomized mice (OVX). And a proteomic analysis suggestes that ALEN-CDs regulate the bone immune microenvironment by affecting mitochondrial metabolism, especially oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS). In conclusion, ALEN-CDs could directly inhibit osteoclast differentiation and regulate the bone immune microenvironment and may be a promising drug for OP treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characteristics of ALEN-CDs. A) TEM image of ALEN-CDs, the white scale bar indicates 20 nm. HRTEM (inset) image, the white scale indicates 1 nm. B) Size distribution of ALEN-CDs. C) XPS of ALEN-CDs. D) High-resolution C1S spectrum. E) High-resolution O1S spectrum. F) High-resolution P 2P spectrum. G) The FTIR spectra of PEG, ALEN-CDs, and ALEN. H) UV–vis absorbance spectra. I) PL spectrum of ALEN-CDs.

Cell cytocompatibility of ALEN-CDs. A) CCK-8 analysis of the viability of BMSCs treated with different ALEN-CDs and ALEN concentrations for 24 h. n = 3, **P < 0.01. B) Live-dead staining of BMSCs incubated with different ALEN-CDs concentrations for 24 h. The white scale bar indicates 50 µm. C) CCK-8 analysis of viability of RAW264.7 cells treated with different ALEN-CDs and ALEN concentrations for 24 h. n = 3, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001. D) Live-dead staining of RAW264.7 cells incubated with different ALEN-CDs concentrations for 24 h. The white scale bar indicates 50 µm. E) Growth curves of BMSCs cultured in 5 µg mL−1 ALEN-CDs and ALEN for 7 days. n = 3.

ALEN-CDs could avoid BRONJ. A) Representative images of wound healing of the tooth extraction and B) micro-CT images of the extraction sockets in normal mice (CONTROL group), BRONJ mice (ALEN group), and ALEN-CDs group at 2 weeks after the tooth extraction. The red dotted line represented tooth extraction wounds. C) HE staining of tooth extraction. D) IHC of CD206, CD80 and TNF-α was performed at tooth extraction. E) Quantification of parameters of BV/TV and Tb.N in each group of mice. F) Quantification of CD206,CD80 and TNF-α positive cells. The black bar indicates 200 µm.

ALEN-CDs suppressed osteoclast differentiation and maturation of BMDMs. A) Representative images of F-actin bands in BMDMs treated with ALEN and ALEN-CDs for 5 days. The white scale bar indicates 50 µm. B) Representative images of TRAP staining in BMDMs treated with ALEN and ALEN-CDs for 5 days. C) Quantitative analysis of the number of F-actin and the area of TRAP-positive osteoclasts ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001. D) The expression level of osteoclast-related genes (CTSK, C-fos, MMP9, and Nfatc1) of BMDMs at 5 days.

Macrophage polarization of RAW264.7 cells under ALEN-CDs treatment for 48 h. A) The expression level of M1-phenotype-related genes (INOS, IL-1β, IL-12) M2-phenotype-related genes (TGF-β, IL-10). n = 3. B) Representative images of IF staining with TNF-α (green) and CD206 (yellow) in RAW264.7 cells. The white bar indicates 10 µm. C) The ratio of M1 macrophages (CD80) and M2 macrophages (CD206) was analyzed using flow cytometry. D) The expression level of macrophage polarization-related proteins, M1 macrophages (CD80), and M2 macrophages (CD206). E) Expression of TNF-α in cell supernatant detected using ELISA analysis. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001.

Evaluation of the effect of ALEN-CDs on OP in ovariectomized-induced mice. A) Schematic illustration of the establishment of the OP model, treatment with drugs, and the evaluation of ALEN-CDs treatment. B) Representative micro-CT images of coronal or distal femurs, cortical bone, and trabecular bone microstructure of ROI after 4 weeks and 8 weeks of treatment. C) Quantification of parameters of BMD, BV/TV, Ct.Th, and Tb.Sp in each group of mice. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001.

Histological evaluation of treatment effect of ALEN-CDs on OP in ovariectomized-induced mice. A) HE staining of femurs after 8 weeks of treatment. B) Representative images of TRAP staining. C) Masson staining of femurs after 8 weeks of treatment. D) IHC of CD206 was performed to analyze the anti-inflammatory of ALEN- CDs in OP. E) IHC of CD80 was performed to analyze the pro-inflammatory of ALEN-CDs in OP. F) Quantification of TRAP positive area in bone area. G) Quantification of CD206 positive cells. H) Quantification of CD80 positive cells. The black bar indicates 200 µm.

Proteomic analysis of the specific mechanism of ALEN-CDs in OP treatment. A) The volcano plot showed significantly upregulated or downregulated proteins (P < 0.05, | Fold change |> 1) in OVX and ALEN-CDs. A total of 91 downregulated proteins were marked in red, and 127 upregulated proteins were marked in green. B) KEGG enrichment analysis of significantly different proteins revealed the top 10 potential pathways that may be involved. C) GO enrichment analysis of significantly different proteins revealed the top 10 terms for BP and CC. D) Differential proteins chord diagram of GO Terms. E) The presentation of the KEGG pathway in OXPHOS. F) The expression level of M1-phenotype-related genes ( IL-1β, IL-12) and M2-phenotype-related genes (TGF-β, IL-10) after rotenone and ALEN-CDs treatment.n=3.

Schematic illustration of the synthesis and OP treatment using ALEN-CDs. ALEN-CDs could directly inhibit osteoclast differentiation and regulate the bone immune microenvironment, thereby alleviating bone loss.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bisphosphonates (BPs) are some of the most widely used drugs in osteoporosis (OP) treatment, but they can cause severe side effects such as bisphosphonate-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (BRONJ), causing great pain in patients. It is necessary to develop a novel drug for treating OP with fewer side effects. In this study, novel carbon dots, ALEN-CDs, derived from polyethylene glycol (PEG) and alendronate (ALEN) are developed. ALEN-CDs have good biocompatibility, photoluminescence (PL) properties, and bone-targeting ability. They can inhibit the differentiation and maturation of osteoclasts, play an immunoregulatory role by inhibiting macrophage polarization to the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype, and promote polarization to the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype. It is worth noting that compared with ALEN, high-dose applications of ALEN-CDs do not lead to BRONJ, which may be related to the regulatory effect on M2 macrophage polarization. Moreover, in vivo studies show that ALEN-CDs significantly reduce bone loss and improve OP in ovariectomized mice (OVX). And a proteomic analysis suggestes that ALEN-CDs regulate the bone immune microenvironment by affecting mitochondrial metabolism, especially oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS). In conclusion, ALEN-CDs could directly inhibit osteoclast differentiation and regulate the bone immune microenvironment and may be a promising drug for OP treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Osteoporosis (OP) is a severe metabolic bone disease caused by an imbalance in bone homeostasis, which is characterized by decreased bone mass, deterioration of bone microstructure, and increased fragility fracture risk. Osteoporotic fractures, in particular hip and spine fractures, often lead to substantial pain and suffering, disability, and even death. As OP often occurs in elderly and postmenopausal women, in an aging society, the health problems caused by OP have become an important issue requiring urgent solutions. The imbalance in bone homeostasis in OP is caused by a disruption in the balance of bone formation by osteoblasts and bone resorption by osteoclasts. Most current OP treatments focus on anti-bone resorption to prevent further bone loss, such as bisphosphonates (BPs) and Denosumab [1]. BPs are the most widely used drugs in OP treatment, and their activity is based on their unique chemical structure, known as the P-C-P backbone structure, which has an extremely strong affinity for hydroxyapatite (HAP) on the bone surface [2]. Alendronate(ALEN) is one of the best and most extensively studied BPs in OP treatment. They can inhibit adenosine triphosphate (ATP) synthesis and block the mevalonate pathway by blocking farnesyl diphosphate synthase to inhibit the formation and function of osteoclasts and induce osteoclast apoptosis [3,4]. In addition to OP, BPs are also widely used in various disease treatments, including Paget’s disease, osteolytic lesions of myeloma, and malignant hypercalcemia. However, it has a long treatment cycle and a slow metabolic process, which can potentially lead to the occurrence of atypical long bone fractures and bisphosphonate-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (BRONJ) [5]. The most severe manifestation among these is BRONJ, characterized by osteonecrosis and bone exposure, leading to significant patient distress. Review studies have shown that the incidence of BRONJ is about 0.12 % in OP patients who receive oral BPs and as high as 7 % in cancer patients who receive intravenous BPs [6]. Therefore, developing novel drugs for treating OP is urgently needed to mitigate side effects and alleviate patient suffering (Scheme 1 ).

Increasing evidence suggests that OP is often accompanied by chronic inflammation and unbalanced macrophage polarization [7]. Macrophages can secrete related cytokines, thereby affecting the bone immune microenvironment and the bone construction process [8,9]. According to surface markers and secreted cytokines, macrophage polarization can be classified as either a pro-inflammatory phenotype (M1) or an anti-inflammatory phenotype (M2) [10]. The change in polarization state mediates the inflammatory response, osteogenesis and osteoclastic differentiation, and vascular regeneration to participate in the bone remodeling process [11]. M1 macrophages can not only induce osteoclast differentiation by secreting inflammatory cytokines but also can differentiate into osteoclasts as precursor cells, contributing to bone resorption [12]. While M2 macrophages mainly produce anti-inflammatory cytokines, such as TGF-β and IL-10. These anti-inflammatory cytokines can inhibit the differentiation and maturation of osteoclasts by acting on receptor activator of nuclear factor kappa-B (RANKL) [12] and can promote osteoblast mineralization, subsequently decreasing bone resorption and increasing bone formation. A large number of studies have shown that cellular metabolism plays an important role in regulating macrophage polarization [13]. Cellular energy metabolism is mainly accomplished by the interrelated pathways of glycolysis, the tricarboxylic acid cycle and OXPHOS [14]. After activation of M1 macrophages, glycolytic predominates in the mitochondrial metabolism to rapidly produce ATP to meet the energy requirements of the inflammatory response, while mitochondrial activity decreases [15]. In the M2 macrophages, the level of oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) in mitochondria is increased and glutamine-fueled mitochondrial respiration is dominant [13]. The M1/M2 ratio reflects the “fluid” state of macrophage polarization, and an imbalance in the M1/M2 ratio will lead to OP. Moreover, recent studies have indicated that the occurrence of BRONJ was always accompanied by a significant increase in M1 macrophages at tooth extraction wounds [16]. BPs can promote macrophages to the M1 macrophage polarization and inhibit M2 phenotype, which is also one of the causes of BRONJ [17,18]. For example, zoledronic acid can increase the expression level of Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR-4), which activates the NF-κB pathway, leads to increased M1 macrophage and decreased M2 polarization in vitro and in vivo [19]. Therefore, modulating macrophage polarization to improve the bone immune microenvironment may provide a new strategy for OP treatment.

Carbon dots (CDs) are nanoparticles with a diameter of 1–10 nm, which have good biocompatibility and stable fluorescence characteristics [20]. They have been widely used in biomedicine and can be used for bioimaging, biosensing, photothermal therapy, and nanomedical applications [21]. Because of their low cost, wide raw material sources, and good biocompatibility, they have been widely applied in the field of nanomedicine, such as the application of J-CDs derived from Jujube in tumor-associated anemia [22], N-CDs derived from chitosan for the treatment of osteolysis induced by Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) or breast cancer [23] and DCDs derived from dexamethasone in bone regeneration [24]. Previous studies have shown that CDs have an immunoregulatory effect, and CDs function is often related to the surface groups [25]. CDs prepared from carob molasses have an inhibitory effect on M1-phenotype macrophage polarization and can inhibit TNF-α and IL-6 cytokine production in macrophages. Dexamethasone carbon dots (DCDs) have been demonstrated to exert immunomodulatory effects in vivo and in vitro. Furthermore, they have been shown to reduce the inflammatory response of bone defects, thereby accelerating bone regeneration [24].

Our previous work synthesized carbon dots with ALEN and polyethyleneimine (PEI), which not only retained the ability of ALEN to inhibit osteoclastogenesis, but also retained the gene-carrying ability of PEI. However, it has the shortcomings of liver accumulation and poor biocompatibility [26]. Polyethylene glycol(PEG) is often used as a passivating agent, which is a non-immunogenic and biocompatible polymer. It is often used for the PEGylation of proteins, peptides and nanoparticles to prolong the circulation time in vivo. In recent years, it has been found that PEG, as a precursor for CDs, can increase the fluorescence property of CDs [27]. It has been suggested that the PEG modified CDs presented an anti-inflammatory effect [25]. In this study, multifunctional CDs derived from ALEN and PEG were developed. The synthesized ALEN-CDs have good biocompatibility, photoluminescence (PL) properties, and bone-targeting ability. In vitro studies have shown that ALEN-CDs can inhibit the differentiation and maturation of osteoclasts and regulate macrophage polarization by inhibiting macrophage polarization to the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype and promoting macrophage polarization to the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype. The ovariectomized mice (OVX) model was established to evaluate the therapeutic effect on OP, and the results showed that ALEN-CDs can significantly alleviate bone loss and improve OP. Moreover, it is worth noting that compared with ALEN, the high-dose application of ALEN-CDs did not lead to BRONJ, which may be related to the regulatory effect of ALEN-CDs on macrophage polarization. Furthermore, a proteomic analysis suggested that ALEN-CDs regulated the bone immune microenvironment by affecting mitochondrial metabolism, especially oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS). Taken together, the ALEN-CDs synthesized in this study may be a promising nanodrug for OP treatment.

According to the mass ratio of 2:1, ALEN (600 mg) and PEG (300 mg) were dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL), mixed evenly, and then heated in a 400 W microwave for 3 min and at 700 W for 10 min. The reaction was stopped when the powder turned a burnt yellow color. The reactant was cooled to room temperature, dissolved in water, and collected. The impurities were removed using filtration-centrifugation, the unreacted small molecule compounds were removed with a 3500 kDa dialysis membrane for 72 h, and the purified ALEN-CDs were obtained. A quantitative ALEN-CDs solution was freeze-dried and weighed, and the ALEN-CDs concentration was obtained.

The morphology of ALEN-CDs was observed and recorded using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, USA). The surface groups of ALEN-CDs, PEG, and ALEN were tested using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Fisher, USA) and UV–VIS absorption. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher, USA) was mainly used to characterize the composition of elements on the surface of ALEN-CDs, especially the elements C, N, O, and P. The optical properties of ALEN-CDs were mainly analyzed using PL.

RAW264.7 or BMSCs were seeded into 96-well plates (3000 cells/well) and incubated in a cell incubator overnight. Then, they were treated with ALEN or ALEN-CDs at increasing concentrations (5, 10, 20, 50, or 100 µg mL−1).

After 24 h, the medium was discarded, and the cells were washed twice with PBS. Then, the cells were incubated with Calcein AM/PI working solution (Beyotime Biotechnology, CHINA) at 37 °C in the dark for 30 min. After staining, the cells were washed with PBS. Subsequently, the images were observed and photographed using an inverted fluorescent microscope (Nikon, ECLIPSE Ti, JAPAN).

After 24 h, the medium was discarded, and 100 µL culture medium containing 10 % CCK-8 was added to the 96-well plate. Following an incubation at 37 °C for 3 h in dark, OD value was detected using a microplate reader (Bio Rad, Japan) at a wavelength of 490 nm.

The accumulative toxicity of the cells was measured as above, but BMSCs were seeded at a density of 1 × 103 cells per well, and the complete culture medium containing 5 µg mL−1 ALEN-CDs or ALEN was replaced every other day, and cell viability was measured daily by CCK-8 method.

This study has been approved by the Ethics Committee (NO.YJSDW2022-230) of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Harbin Medical University. A total of 15 six-week-old female C57BL/6 mice were purchased from Animal Experiment Center of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Harbin Medical University. They were randomly divided into 3 groups: CONTROL, ALEN and ALEN-CDs. The mice in ALEN group were injected intravenously with ALEN (125 µg kg−1) twice a week and dexamethasone (DEX, 5 mg kg−1) once a week for 4 weeks [16,28], while ALEN-CDs groups were treated with ALEN-CDs and DEX at the same dose and frequency. At the same time, CONTROL groups were given PBS intravenously. All mice underwent tooth extraction at the second week and were continually administered with drugs for 2 weeks. After 4 weeks of administration, the mice were anesthetized and the maxillae were harvested for micro-CT scanning.

The maxillae were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde (PFA) for 48 h, and then were scanned using micro-CT (Scanco, Switzerland). After scanning, the region of interest (ROI) of tooth extraction sockets was reconstructed with 3D software, and various bone tissue parameters were analyzed, including bone volume fraction (BV/TV) and trabecular number (Tb.N).

After micro-CT scanning, the maxillae were put into a beaker containing 15 % EDTA decalcifying solution on a magnetic stirrer and the solution was replaced every two days until the decalcification was complete. After decalcification, the tissues were washed under running water for 12 h, dehydrated in automatic dehydration machine (Thermo scientific, STP 120, USA and embedded. The sections were sliced with 3 µm thickness using manual rotation microtome (Thermo scientific, MICROM HM325, USA) for further histological staining. Hematoxylin and eosin staining (HE) were performed on the sections according to the instructions. After staining, the slides were observed and photographed with an upright microscope (Nikon eclipse 80i, JAPAN).

The maxillae sections were routine dewaxed to water, and antigen was retrieved with high pressure method. After endogenous peroxidase activity and nonspecific antigens was blocked, the sections were incubated at room temperature for 2 h with primary antibody (CD80, CD206, TNF-α, 1:100, Proteintech, CHINA). The second antibody was then incubated, followed by DAB staining (ZSGB-BIO, CHINA) according to manual and counterstaining with methylgreen. After sealing, the slides were observed and photographed with an upright microscope.

Whole blood was collected from the inner canthal vein of mice, which was placed in a heparin lithium anticoagulant tube, thoroughly mixed. And 1 mL of whole blood was gently mixed with 9 mL 0.9 % sodium chloride solution, and then centrifuged (1500 rpm, 10 min). After centrifugation, the supernatant was abandoned and erythrocytes were retained, and then erythrocytes were washed with 0.9 % sodium chloride solution until the supernatant was colorless. 400 µL erythrocytes were mixed with 10 mL 0.9 % sodium chloride solution to prepare 4 % erythrocyte suspension. 4 % erythrocyte suspension was mixed with different concentrations of ALEN-CDs (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 µg mL−1) and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. After incubation, the supernatant was collected by centrifugation (2500 rpm, 10 min) and OD value was detected using a microplate reader at a wavelength of 540 nm.

RAW264.7, MC3T3-E1 and BMSCs were placed in Cell Culture and Imaging Microplates (96 Wells, Agilent, USA) with 5000 cells per well. After incubating overnight in an incubator, ALEN-CDs (100 µL of 50 µg mL−1 solution) were added to each well. It was incubated in a live cell imaging system (BioTek Cytation, USA) containing 5 % CO2 at 37 °C for 4 h, and the uptake process was observed and photographed in real time.

In vitro: ALEN-CDs (100 µg mL−1, 1 mL) were mixed with hydroxyapatite (HAP, 100 mg), and incubated overnight on a shaker. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation (1000 rpm, 5 min), which OD value of supernatant was measured by a fluorescent enzyme spectrometer, and the adsorption rate was calculated.

In vivo: 1 mg/mL ALEN-CDs or PBS were intravenously injected into 6 weeks C57BL/6 mice via the tail vein. After 72 h, the mice were anesthetized, and observed by an in vivo imaging system (Bruker). Then the mandible, bone and main organs (such as bone, liver, and kidney) were also observed.

BMDMs (bone marrow derived macrophages) were isolated from femur and tibia of male C57BL/6 mice aged 6 weeks. They were seeded into plates at density of 1 × 105 cells mL−1, and complete medium containing 40 ng mL−1 M−CSF (Amizon, USA) was used. After culture for 24 h, 40 ng mL−1 RANKL (Amizon, USA) was added to each well to induce osteoclastic differentiation. After two days of induction, the complete medium containing 40 ng mL−1 M−CSF and RANKL was replaced. Osteoclasts could be observed the next day.

After induction, the medium was discarded. The cells were washed with distilled water, fixed with 4 %PFA for 20 min, and then washed for 3 times. Then we added 0.1 % Triton X-100 to each well to permeate cytomembrane at room temperature for 15 min, and cleaned for 3 times. According to instruction, the TRAP work solution (Servicebio, CHINA) was prepared and added to the cells, which was incubated at 37 °C for 20 min in the dark. After incubation, the cells were washed for 3 times. The cells were observed and photographed with a microscope.

After induced, fixed, permeated and washed, the cells were incubated with phalloidin (MesGen, CHINA) at room temperature for 60 min in dark. After incubation, the cells were washed with PBS and treated with DAPI for 3 min.Then the cells were washed, added anti-fluorescence quencher and were observed with a microscope.

After cells were treated and cleaned by PBS, total RNA of the cells was isolated using TRIZOL (TAKARA, JAPAN). cDNA was reverse-transcribed from 900 ng RNA and was amplified to collect fluorescence signal using PCR system (Mx3005p, Agilent, USA). The primer sequences used in the experiment are shown in Table 1 . The relative expression of gene expression level was calculated by 2−ΔΔ
Ct method.

In order to assess the effect of ALEN-CDs on the polarization of macrophages, RAW264.7 cells were seeded in 6-well plates, cultured overnight, and treated with ALEN-CDs or ALEN for 48 h. Following this, RNA and protein were extracted from the cells in order to evaluate the expression levels of macrophage-related genes and proteins, and the culture medium was collected in order to assess the effect on secretory protein of macrophages.

In order to verify whether ALEN-CDs affect macrophage polarization through oxidative phosphorylation, RAW264.7 cells were treated with 1 µm rotenone for 24 h. Following this, the cells were treated with complete medium or 10 µg mL−1ALEN-CDs for 48 h. Then RNA of the cells was extracted to evaluate the expression levels of macrophage-related genes.

After treatment for 48 h, culture medium was collected, centrifuged (500g, 5 min), and then the supernatant was taken and stored at −80 °C for further analysis. In the enzyme label plate, the sample, standard substance or sample diluent was respectively added to the sample well, standard well or control well, respectively. The plate was then incubated at 37 °C for 90 min. After incubation, the plate was dried and washed with washing solution for 3 times. Then antibody working solution was added to each well, which the plate was incubated at 37 °C for 60 min. After the washing steps, streptavidin, color reagent, and termination solution were added in sequence. After termination of the reaction, the absorbance was performed with a microplate reader at a wavelength of 450 nm.

After cells were treated and cleaned with PBS, the total protein of the cells was isolated. The protein loading amount was 30 µg, and gel electrophoresis was performed at a constant voltage of 80 V. After electrophoresis, the protein was transferred to a PVDF membrane with a constant current of 300 mA. After transfer, the membrane was washed and blocked with 5 % skim milk at room temperature for 90 min.Then PVDF membrane was incubated with primary antibody (CD80, CD206 1:100, Proteintech, CHINA and GAPDH 1:5000, CST, USA). The secondary antibodies of the corresponding species were incubated at room temperature for 90 min. ECL enhanced chemiluminescence was used for chromogenic reaction.

RAW264.7 was seeded at cell slides and was treated with ALEN-CDs or ALEN for 48 h. After washed, fixed, permeated and blocked, the cells were incubated with primary antibody (TNF-α, CD206, 1:100, Proteintech, CHINA) at 4 °C overnight. On the second day, corresponding fluorescent secondary antibodies were added for incubation. After staining, Antifade Mounting Medium was added to seal the slides. The slides were observed and taken by confocal microscopy.

After cells were treated and cleaned by PBS, they were collected by centrifuging at 1000 rpm for 5 min and were resuspended by staining buffer to the density of the cells to 1 × 107 mL−1. Each marker was detected by flow cytometry with single stain method. For surface marker APC-CD80, the experiment steps are as follows. Except negative control group, each group was added with 5 µL APC-CD80 and incubated at 4 °C for 30 min in the dark. Then the cells were centrifuged, washed and resuspended by staining buffer. The cells were tested by flow cytometry.

For intracellular marker FITC-CD206, the experiment steps are as follows. Fixation/Permeabilization working solution was prepared using deionized water in proportion and the solution was added to cells, which was incubated at room temperature for 30 min. Then the cells were washed by cell membrane permeabilization wash buffer and incubated with FITC-CD206 at 4 °C for 30 min in the dark. Finally, the cells were centrifuged, washed and resuspended by staining buffer. The cells were tested by flow cytometry.

A total of 30 female C57BL/6 mice aged 8 weeks were used in this study, which were purchased from Animal Experiment Center of The Second Affiliated Hospital of Harbin Medical University. They were randomly divided into 5 groups: Sham group, OVX (ovariectomy) group, OVX + ALEN group, OVX + low ALEN-CDs group and OVX + high ALEN-CDs group. After abdominal anesthesia, mice underwent an ovariectomy as described elsewhere to induce osteoporosis [29,30]. After 6 weeks, the mouse femur was evaluated using X-ray to confirm whether the osteoporosis model was successfully established. After successful modeling, the Sham and OVX groups were injected intravenously with 200 μL PBS. The ALEN group was injected with 200 μL of 1 mg/mL ALEN, the L ALEN-CDs group was injected with 200 μL of 1 mg/mL ALEN-CDs, and the H ALEN-CDs group was injected with 200 μL of 2 mg/mL ALEN-CDs. The frequency of administration was once a week. After 4 weeks or 8 weeks of administration, the mice were anesthetized and the femurs were harvested for next step.

The femurs were fixed and then scanned using micro-CT. After scanning, ROI was reconstructed with 3D software, and various bone tissue parameters were analyzed, including bone volume fraction (BV/TV), bone mineral density (BMD), Cortical bone thickness (Ct.Th) and trabecular spacing (Tb.Sp). After micro-CT scanning, the fixed femurs were decalcified, dehydrated and embedded. HE staining, TRAP staining, Masson staining (Solarbio, CHINA) and Immumohistochemical staining were performed on the femur sections according to the instructions. After staining, the slides were observed and photographed with an upright microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biological safety elevation of ALEN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The major organs, such as heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney, were fixed with 4 % PFA for 24 h, then flushed, dehydrated, embedded and sliced according to the above steps to evaluate the biosecurity of ALEN-CDs. And HE staining were performed.

Whole blood was collected from the inner canthal vein of mice, which was placed in a heparin lithium anticoagulant tube, thoroughly mixed, centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 15 min, and then supernatant was taken, transferred to a clean centrifuge tube, and stored at −80 °C for future use. Liver and kidney function is tested using Mindray’s fully automated biochemical analyzer (Mindray, BS-120, CHINA) to assess biosafety.

The proteomic was conducted by Nanjing Yiwei Jianhua Biotechnology, were tested to analyze the specific mechanism of ALEN-CDs in the treatment of osteoporosis mice. Firstly, the femur protein was extracted and subjected to quality control. After digestion, 500 ng peptide segment was used and separated by chromatographic system (EASY- nLC 1200, thermo scientific, USA). DIA mass spectrometry was then performed using a spectrometer (QExactive HF-X, thermo scientific, USA). After the analysis, data were processed. The Pulsar in Spectronaunt software was applied to establish the atlas library using DDA and DIA, and then quantitative data were processed and Limma was used for differential expression analysis. The screening criteria were P < 0.05 and the fold change > 1, and the results of differently expressed proteins were obtained after screening. Then KEGG and GO analysis were performed.

Graphpad Prism 9.0 software was used for statistical analysis. The data were presented as mean ± standard deviation (Mean ± SD). One-way ANOVA and t-test were used to analyze data, which P < 0.05 indicated statistical significance.

In this study, ALEN-CDs were synthesized from ALEN and PEG using the microwave method for the first time. The morphology of ALEN-CDs was analyzed using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, ALEN-CDs were successfully synthesized and were spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 1.48 ± 0.58 nm (Fig. 1B). They were uniformly dispersed in water without obvious agglomeration, and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) results showed that they had clear lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to graphite- carbon (100).

Then, the surface groups were detected using XPS analysis, FTIR, and UV–visible absorption spectroscopy. The XPS results (Fig. 1C) showed that ALEN-CDs were mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and phosphorus. Fig. 1D of the high-resolution C1S spectrum showed three peaks, 287.8 eV, 286.2 eV [31] and 284.8 eV [22], corresponding to CO, C–O, and C–H/C–C, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectrum as Fig. 1E showed C–O–H/C–O–C (532.8 eV), and CO (531 eV). The characteristic peaks of chemical nitrogen were fitted to two main peaks at 401.3 eV and 399.6 eV, respectively, representing graphite nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen (N-H and C-N) (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 1G, the FTIR results showed that ALEN-CDs had a wide characteristic absorption peak at 3417 cm−1, corresponding to the N-H and O-H stretching vibration peaks of 3500–3300 cm−1, which were mainly derived from the amino and hydroxyl groups in ALEN and PEG. The peak value at 2936 cm−1 corresponds to the C-H stretching vibration peak from 3000 to 2843 cm−1. These characteristic absorption peaks indicated the presence of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of ALEN-CDs, which are mainly derived from ALEN and PEG. Moreover, ALEN-CDs retained the ALEN phosphate group, which was mainly manifested as a PO bending vibration peak at 1300–1200 cm−1, P-O symmetric bending vibration peak at 956 cm−1, and P-O antisymmetric bending vibration peak near 1053 cm−1, which was also confirmed by XPS (Fig. 1F). The UV–vis absorption spectrum showed there was an obvious band of medium and low intensity at 250–300 nm, which was mainly due to the n-π* transition in the sp2 domains (Fig. 1H), indicating ALEN-CDs contain unsaturated heteroatoms [32]. These results indicated that we had successfully synthesized ALEN-CDs, which retained the characteristic functional group of ALEN.

CDs are often used in biomedical fields such as biological imaging because of their excellent fluorescence properties. To further explore the optical properties of ALEN-CDs, PL was applied. The PL spectrum showed that ALEN-CDs had superior optical properties with excitation dependence, which was a common phenomenon in CDs (Fig. 1I). The PL spectrum showed that when the excitation wavelength was 360 nm, it could produce a strong PL emission peak in the range of 400–500 nm, and the maximum value was about 440 nm. The emission spectrum was redshifted with an increase in excitation wavelength, which was related to quantum size effects and surface energy traps.

The excellent optical and structural properties of ALEN-CDs indicated that they may have value for biological applications. The prerequisite for nanoparticles to play a therapeutic role is cellular uptake, primarily through endocytosis. To assess whether ALEN-CDs could be taken up by cells, ALEN-CDs were co-cultured with RAW264.7, MC3T3-E1, and BMSCs cells, which were observed and photographed using a live cell imaging system. Confocal images (Fig. S3) revealed that ALEN-CDs in the cells produced green fluorescence under blue excitation light and red fluorescence under green excitation light, and they were mainly distributed in the cytoplasm after entering the cell but did not enter the nucleus. This phenomenon proved ALEN-CDs had excitation dependence and were successfully taken up by cells. The aggregation of ALEN-CDs around the nucleus may be related to the internalization of nanoparticles into lysosomes and the presence of nuclear pore complexes on the nuclear membrane [33].

We have demonstrated that ALEN-CDs exhibit excellent fluorescence properties, and then, using their fluorescence properties, we further detected their bone targeting. An important prerequisite to the ability of ALEN for OP treatment is its bone-targeting properties, which can bind tightly to HAP. Therefore, it is particularly important to evaluate whether ALEN-CDs have bone-targeting properties in vitro and in vivo. After incubating 100 µg mL−1ALEN-CDs with HAP for 24 h, the absorption ratio was as high as 45 %, which had statistical significance (Fig. S4). In vivo bone-targeting studies (Fig. S5) demonstrated that ALEN-CDs were mainly enriched in the femur, tibia, and jaw bone and were also enriched in other non-trunk bones. The small amount of fluorescence in the control group was due to the self-fluorescence signal in mice, and its presentation showed irregularity. To further detect ALEN-CDs accumulation, the liver, kidneys, jaw bone, and long bone of mice were extracted. It was found that ALEN-CDs were mainly enriched in bone, with a small amount of accumulation in the liver and kidneys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALEN-CDs have good biocompatibility and do not cause BRONJ-like lesions
ce:section.ce:para: Good biocompatibility is a prerequisite for nanomedicine applications. In this study, we used a cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) and live-dead staining to detect the biocompatibility of ALEN-CDs in vitro. As shown in Fig. 2 A, CCK-8 results showed that ALEN-CDs had mild cytotoxicity compared with ALEN in BMSCs. One day after treatment, ALEN-CDs showed low cytotoxicity only at a concentration of 100 µg mL−1, and the cell viability still reached 75 %, which was further confirmed by live-dead staining (Fig. 2B). The BMSCs cell morphology was long spindles and spread well under different ALEN-CDs concentrations. However, ALEN showed cytotoxicity at a lower concentration of 20 µg mL−1, and its survival rate was about 75 %. When the ALEN concentration reached 50 µg mL−1, it significantly inhibited cell proliferation, and the cell viability was less than 40 %, which had statistical significance. ALEN-CDs also showed good biocompatibility in RAW264.7 cells (Fig. 2C), which was also similar in the BMSCs. Live-dead staining results (Fig. 2D) were consistent with the CCK-8 results for RAW264.7, and ALEN-CDs showed mild cytotoxicity only at higher concentrations of 50 µg mL−1 and 100 µg mL−1. It was confirmed that the biocompatibility of nanomedicines is closely related to their size, shape, and surface properties [34–37], and CDs are not cytotoxic in the case of their shape, size, and nanoscale structures. Moreover, CDs cytotoxicity was mainly related to the surface groups, Wang et al [34] found PEG passivated CDs had better biocompatibility. Therefore, PEG was used as a carbon source to synthesize ALEN-CDs in our study, which had good biocompatibility. The side effects of ALEN are mainly related to dose and time accumulation, so the detection of cumulative ALEN-CDs toxicity is particularly important. The cumulative toxicity of ALEN-CDs and ALEN was detected using the CCK-8 method (Fig. 2E). A solution of 5 µg mL−1 ALEN-CDs or ALEN was co-cultured with BMSCs and detected for 7 days. The results showed there was no significant difference in BMSCs after co-incubation with 5 µg mL−1 ALEN-CDs for 7 days, while ALEN showed obvious cumulative cytotoxicity, and the cell survival rate was less than 50 % on day 4. Since anti-OP drugs are also administered intravenously, the compatibility of ALEN-CDs with blood was further investigated. The hemolysis test showed that they had good blood biocompatibility (Fig. S6).

ALEN has been used in clinical treatment for OP due to its properties of bone targeting and osteoclast inhibition [2,3]. However, an increasing number of studies have revealed that ALEN-based treatment could cause BRONJ and seriously affect the physical and mental health of patients, which raised concerns about the application of ALEN. When ALEN is used to treat bone metabolism diseases, dental implantation, periodontal operation, and tooth extraction can all induce BRONJ [5,38], of which tooth extraction is the most common trigger factor for BRONJ [39]. To detect the effect of ALEN-CDs on tooth extraction in vivo, a BRONJ-like lesion was established using intravenous injections of ALEN-CDs or ALEN with tooth extraction in accordance with a previous study [28,40]. The mucosa of tooth extraction in ALEN groups was yellow and white, while the mucosa in the controls was normal, indicating the BRONJ model was successfully conducted (Fig. 3 A). As shown in Fig. 3B, ALEN-CDs significantly promoted wound healing compared with those in the ALEN group. In addition, ALEN-CDs obviously promoted bone formation in extraction sockets, while there was almost no bone formation in the tooth extraction sites of the ALEN group. The quantitative analysis of the region of interest (ROI) in tooth extraction sockets such as BV/TV and Tb.N was conducted, and the results showed a decrease in BV/TV and Tb.N values in the ALEN groups, compared with the control and ALEN-CDs groups (Fig. 3E). These results suggested ALEN-CDs could promote wound healing, especially promoting bone formation, without causing BRONJ-like lesions. Subsequently, in order to investigate the regeneration of soft and bone tissue in the tooth extraction, HE staining was conducted on the wound. The findings demonstrated that in the ALEN group, the gingiva was absent, the jaw was exposed, and there was minimal epithelial coverage, along with the presence of cavities in the necrotic alveolar bone. However, the control and ALEN-CDs tooth extraction wounds demonstrated complete epithelial coverage, as well as normal connective tissue and bone tissue structure. And IHC staining of CD206, CD80 and TNF-α (Fig. 3D,3F) revealed a locally alleviated inflammatory response on the bone surface of the healed extraction sockets in the ALEN-CDs group, in comparison to the ALEN group. The findings indicate that ALEN-CDs may be an effective means of preventing BRONJ. It is postulated that this may be due to their ability to regulate macrophage polarization.

In the bone-targeting experiment (Fig. S5), we found a small accumulation of ALEN-CDs in the liver and kidneys. The kidneys and liver are the main nanoparticle metabolism pathways. Renal excretion is the ideal way to metabolize nanoparticles, and its metabolic pathway is closely related to nanoparticle size. Nanoparticles with a particle size of less than 6 nm can be filtered through the glomerulus and eliminated in the urine. The size of ALEN-CDs was 1–2 nm, so they were mainly metabolized by the kidneys [41]. The liver is the main pathway of metabolism for nanoparticles that are not cleared by the kidneys. Due to the small particle size of ALEN-CDs, most were metabolized in the kidneys, while the liver metabolized a few, so the accumulation in the liver is relatively minor. The kidneys mainly metabolize the nanoparticles, where cumulative toxicity is difficult, which may be related to ALEN-CDs not leading to BRONJ. The results showed that ALEN-CDs had good biocompatibility, and the application of ALEN-CDs can alleviate the occurrence of BRONJ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALEN-CDs inhibit osteoclast differentiation and osteoclast maturation of BMDMs
ce:section.ce:para: In the early stage, our team synthesized CDs using ALEN and PEI and found that PEI-CDs retained the properties of ALEN, which could directly inhibit osteoclast differentiation [26]. Osteoclasts play an important role in bone remodeling through bone resorption. Osteoclasts originate from bone marrow monocytes and differentiate into osteoclasts under the dual stimulation of macrophage colony-stimulating factor (M-CSF) and RANKL. To test whether ALEN-CDs have an inhibitory effect on osteoclast differentiation, we used M-CSF and RANKL to induce BMDMs to differentiate into mature osteoclasts, with ALEN or ALEN-CDs treatment for 5 days. F-ACTIN staining results (Fig. 4 A) showed ALEN-CDs, and ALEN could significantly inhibit the formation of F-actin bands in mature osteoclasts, suggesting that ALEN-CDs could suppress osteoclast maturation. As shown in tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase (TRAP) staining (Fig. 4B), more multinuclear giant cells could be seen in the positive group, and the TRAP staining was positive, indicating the successful induction of osteoclast differentiation.

The results suggested that ALEN-CDs had a significant inhibiting effect on osteoclast differentiation. Statistical analysis of the number of F-actin and the area of TRAP-positive osteoclasts revealed a significant reduction in mature osteoclasts in the ALEN or ALEN-CDs groups compared with the positive group. The differences were statistically significant (Fig. 4C).

Osteoclasts are multinucleated giant cells derived from monocytes/macrophages [42]. Mature osteoclasts pass through multiple stages, from formation to differentiation. First, macrophages differentiate into osteoclast precursor cells under the stimulation of M-CSF. In this early stage, the expression level of the nuclear factor of activated T cells 1 (Nfatc1) and Fos proto-oncogene (C-fos) increase. Second, under the synergistic stimulation of M-CSF and RANKL, the precursor cells fuse to form actin rings and fold edges. Then, matrix metallopeptidase 9 (MMP9), cathepsin K (CTSK), and TRAP are secreted to play the role of bone absorption, which allows for osteoclast maturation [26,43]. To investigate the effects of ALEN-CDs on osteoclastic differentiation further, the expression levels of osteoclast-related genes (CTSK, C-fos, Nfatc1, and MMP9) were detected using RT-qPCR. The results showed that the expression levels of osteoclast-related genes in 5 µg mL−1 ALEN, 5 µg mL−1, and 10 µg mL−1 ALEN-CDs groups were significantly lower than those in the positive group. These results indicated that ALEN-CDs had a significant inhibitory effect on osteoclastogenesis, especially at the early and maturation stages. The mRNA levels of osteoclast-related genes in the 10 µg mL−1 ALEN-CDs group were lower than those in the 5 µg mL−1 ALEN-CDs group, and the above results were statistically significant (Fig. 4D), indicating the inhibitory effect of ALEN-CDs on osteoclast differentiation was concentration-dependent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALEN-CDs inhibit M1 macrophage polarization and promote M2 macrophage polarization in RAW264.7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Macrophages have been considered important regulators of bone homeostasis through adjustment of macrophage polarization in OP. The M1/M2 ratio was essential for OP treatment [44,45]. Previous studies have shown that CDs with different surface modifications had different immunomodulatory effects [25]. To further investigate the effect of ALEN-CDs on immunomodulatory, RAW264.7 cells were treated with ALEN-CDs or ALEN for 48 h. RT-qPCR (Fig. 5 A) results demonstrated that ALEN-CDs significantly promoted the expression of IL-10 and TGF-β (M2-phenotype-related genes) and suppressed the expression of IONS, IL-1β, IL-12 (M1-phenotype related genes). Next, to further confirm the regulatory effect of ALEN-CDs on macrophage polarization, immunofluorescence (IF), flow cytometry, and Western blots were conducted. These results indicated that there was more expression of M2-phenotype-related proteins, such as CD206, and decreased expression of M1-phenotype-related proteins in the ALEN-CDs group, such as TNF-α (Fig. 5B) and CD80 (Fig. 5C-D). Moreover, ELISA analysis revealed that ALEN-CDs significantly inhibited the secretion of TNF-α into the cellular supernatant (Fig. 5E). These results demonstrated that ALEN-CDs could induce macrophages to polarize from the M0 to M2 phenotype and improve the immune microenvironment. Macrophage polarization not only plays an important role in the regulation of the immune microenvironment of OP but is also related to the pathogenesis of BRONJ. Previous studies have shown BRONJ was always accompanied by a significant increase in M1 phenotype macrophages and increased M1/M2 ratio at the tooth extraction wound, which may be the cause of BRONJ [16]. In our results, ALEN significantly promoted the of M1-phenotype-related genes and had obvious pro-inflammatory effects, while ALEN had no obvious effect on M2-phenotype-related genes, and ALEN-CDs had significant anti-inflammatory effects. The anti-inflammatory properties of ALEN-CDs may be the reason why BRONJ is less likely to occur in ALEN-CDs groups. Compared with ALEN, ALEN-CDs not only retained the inhibitory effect on osteoclastogenesis but also had an immunomodulatory effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of ALEN-CDs on OP in ovariectomized-induced mice
ce:section.ce:para: Having proved that ALEN-CDs had the function of inhibiting osteoclastic differentiation and regulating macrophage polarization in vitro, we further explored the role of ALEN-CDs in OP treatment in OVX according to the timeline, as shown in Fig. 6 A. After 4 and 8 weeks of treatment with ALEN and ALEN-CDs, we conducted micro-CT scanning of the femurs. The 3D reconstruction results showed a significant bone loss in the bone trabecula in the OVX groups compared with the Sham groups. However, ALEN and ALEN-CDs mitigated bone loss and notably increased bone mass (Fig. 6B). The quantitative analysis of the ROI in trabecular parameters such as BV/TV, BMD, and Tb.Sp and bone cortex paraments (Ct.Th) was conducted; these parameters could reflect changes in bone mass. The results showed that an increase in the BV/TV, BMD, and Ct.Th values and a decrease in Tb.Sp in the ALEN and ALEN-CDs groups, and the values approached those in the Sham groups, suggesting ALEN-CDs had effective therapeutic effects on OP (Fig. 6C).

Histological staining further demonstrated the effect of ALEN-CDs on relieving osteoporosis. HE staining (Fig. 7 A) revealed that the thickness of trabecular bone and growth plate in the OVX group was significantly lower than that of the Sham group, while the thickness of trabecular bone in the other treatment groups was significantly higher than that of the OVX group. ALEN and ALEN-CDs exhibited a clear therapeutic effect on osteoporosis. Osteoclasts are important cells in the bone remodeling process, and TRAP-positive cells on the trabecular surface indicate that they are mature osteoclasts and play a role in bone absorption. TRAP staining revealed that ALEN-CDs significantly inhibited the number of osteoclasts, and the effect was similar in the ALEN group (Fig. 7B and F). Masson staining further confirmed that ALEN-CDs could improve bone metabolism, in which the growth plate was obviously thickened, and most tissues in ALEN-CDs were blue-stained, indicating that bone remodeling was active (Fig. 7C). It has been reported that OP is usually accompanied by chronic inflammation. In OVX mice, serum inflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α and IL-17, were increased. Meanwhile, the anti-inflammation cytokine concentration was reduced [46]. To investigate the anti-inflammatory activity of ALEN-CDs in OP, an immunohistochemical (IHC) analysis was performed. The IHC results showed that the expression of CD206 in the ALEN-CDs group was higher than in other groups, while the expression of CD80 was decreased. Compared with the Sham group, the expression of CD206 was inhibited in the OVX and ALEN groups, and CD80 was promoted (Fig. 7D–E and G-H). The results revealed that ALEN-CDs could regulate the bone immune microenvironment by inhibiting polarization to the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype and promoting polarization to the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype. Taken together, these results suggested that ALEN-CDs could alleviate bone loss in OP by immunomodulation and inhibition of osteoclastogenesis. It has been shown that ALEN-CDs had a good therapeutic effect on OP in vitro and in vivo experiments. In order to evaluate the safety of ALEN-CDs in vivo, HE staining and hematological analysis were performed. HE staining results revealed that there was no significant difference in vital organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidneys) among the experimental groups. (Fig. S7A). Next, the hematological analysis showed that there were no notable changes in the values of CREA-SOX, UREA, AST, and ALT, as they all fell within the normal range (Fig. S7B). However, the value of ALP was significantly decreased in the OVX group compared with other groups. The ALP level in serum is not only a criterion for liver health but also an auxiliary criterion for OP diagnosis [47,48]. Because ALP was closely related to bone formation as a byproduct of osteoblasts, the ALP level in our experiments also confirmed through serology that ALEN-CDs had a positive effect on OP treatment. Taken together, these results demonstrated that ALEN-CDs enhanced bone mass and had good biosecurity in OP mice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALEN-CDs regulate macrophage polarization through mitochondrial metabolism
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, proteomics was performed to identify differentially expressed proteins (DEPs) in the OVX and H ALEN-CDs groups. The proteomics results showed that ALEN-CDs groups contained 218 DEPs, including 91 downregulated and 127 upregulated proteins, compared with the OVX group (Fig. 8 A, S8). Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway enrichment analysis was performed on DEPs (Fig. 8B). In the KEGG analysis of DEPs, the OXPHOS signaling pathway was observed. This suggested that OP treatment with ALEN-CDs may be related to OXPHOS, which was closely associated with M2-type macrophages [49]. Plentiful studies have shown that changes in cellular immune metabolism may be an important factor in regulating macrophage polarization [13]. Macrophages are considered metabolically active cells and produce ATP mainly through OXPHOS, which occurs in the mitochondria. The immunometabolism of M2 macrophages was characterized by the up-regulation of OXPHOS levels and glutamine-fueled mitochondrial respiration, in which uridine diphosphate N-acetylglucosamine (UDP- GlcNAc) played an important role [50,51]. However, after the activation of M1 macrophages, glycolysis predominates in the mitochondrial metabolism to rapidly produce ATP to meet the energy requirements of the inflammatory response, while was mainly mediated by hypoxia-inducible factor 1-α (HIF-1α) [15]. In this process, the OXPHOS level in mitochondria and electron transport chain (ETC) activity were inhibited [52], accompanied by the expression of pro-inflammatory genes. The above research findings confirmed the immune environment was closely related to mitochondrial metabolism, especially OXPHOS.

Top10 Biological Process (BP) and Cellular Component (CC) analysis of Gene Ontology (GO) also revealed significantly different proteins between the OVX and ALEN-CDs groups associated with mitochondrial metabolism, particularly enriched in OXPHOS (Fig. 8C). As shown in Fig. 8D, a differential protein chord diagram was constructed to display the proteins enriched in each GO Term and their upregulated and downregulated situation. In GO Terms, the expression of TGFβ-1 and IGF-1 in ALEN-CDs was upregulated, while NF-κB was downregulated (Fig. S9), which this phenomenon suggested that ALEN-CDs for OP treatment is closely related to the regulation of macrophage polarization consistent with our vitro experiments. Moreover, OXPHOS-related proteins were significantly upregulated, such as Ndufa11, Ndufa3, Ndufb9, Ndufb11, COX6A, and COX7B (Fig. 8D, 8E, S10). Mitochondrial chain complex I, mainly located at the inner mitochondrial membrane, was also detected among the DEPs, which was associated with ATP production through OXPHOS [53]. Rotenone, a mitochondrial chain complex I inhibitor, can significantly inhibit the oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) level of cells [54]. Therefore, we treated cells with rotenone as an OXPHOS inhibitor for 24 h and subsequently treated them with ALEN-CDs for 48 h, after which we analyzed the expression of genes associated with polarization. The results (Fig. 8F) demonstrated that the expression levels of M1 polarization-related genes, including IL-1β and IL-12, were significantly elevated. Whereas the expression levels of M2 polarization-related genes, such as IL-10 and TGF-β, were significantly reduced following treatment with rotenone. And ALEN-CDs were unable to reverse this trend. However, when the cells were not treated with rotenone and only treated with ALEN-CDs, the expression levels of M1 polarization-related genes decreased, while the expression levels of M2 polarization-related genes increased. These results confirmed that ALEN-CDs affected the cell polarization state through OXPHOS. Therefore, the effect of ALEN-CDs on alleviating bone loss may be mediated by regulating mitochondrial metabolism, especially OXPHOS.

In summary, we developed a novel nanodrug (ALEN-CDs) based on ALEN and evaluated its therapeutic effects and mechanisms on OP. The prepared ALEN-CDs not only retain the bone-targeting and anti-bone resorption properties of ALEN but also exert anti-inflammatory effects by regulating macrophage polarization. More importantly, ALEN-CDs can effectively treat OP and avoid side effects, such as BRONJ. Mechanistically, the immunomodulatory effect of ALEN-CDs may be through the regulation of mitochondrial OXPHOS. Based on the above results, the synthesized ALEN-CDs can effectively treat OP with minimal side effects and have the potential to become a promising alternative drug for ALEN.
